transcript.transcript: Okay. All right. All right. My name is Randa George, and I'm volunteering with the Emmett Orchard's oral history project here in Emmett, Idaho. And today is Sunday, June 15th, 2025. And I'm with Sue Gunter, um, who was born and raised here in Emmett. And she has a lot of stories to tell about Emmett Orchards and growing up in the city and the town area around Emmett. Um, and she's going to share those today with us. This oral history is conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society and is part of the America 250 in Idaho. Um, commemoration of the, uh, the centennial. Um, Sue, I'd like to thank you for, um, spending time today sharing your personal stories. And this interview will allow you to reflect and share your memories and experiences about the area of Emmett. Um, I assume I can call you Sue. Um, but before we start with any specific questions about the orchards, and you're growing up here in Emmett, do you give permission to record this interview? So Sue's the main one and and the focus today. So I'd like to start with some general questions about my narrator today. Um, if you can, please tell me when and where you were born and a little bit about your early life. If you've got any memories that date back to that time. I was born and raised in Emmett, Idaho. Um, there was a small hospital called Mary Secord. That's now Walter Knox. First and now even better health. But it was right where the old fire station is located. That is where I was born. Um. And their names were. Alan and Sherry. And Sherry was the youngest. Sherry is the youngest. Okay. Can you tell me a little bit about who specifically your parents were. Um, my mother was Evelyn Mae Walker. She was born in, um, Boise at Saint Luke's. Her father was Clarence Lawrence Walker, who was a superintendent in, uh, the barber mill outside of Boise. My father was Floyd David Brown. He was the second boy in the family. Um, of Hazel and Alan Brown. And they lived out on the South Slope. He was born in Emmett. And, um, they had, uh, taken over the house that he, uh, grew up in and that we all call, uh, the family home. He, uh, was a part of, uh, that family, uh, of Uncle Charlie, um, who provided for Grandma Brown as long as she took care of him than she inherited the house and the orchard. So, um, dad did several things in his lifetime. One of them was to manage the the orchards while his father, uh, received cancer treatment, and his other two brothers were serving in the military at that time. Okay, um, let's get back to you specifically. Um, is there any particular story about your your given name? Um, were you named after a family member, for example, and who gave your name, or did you have any nicknames? Well, I always had a nickname, some of them better than others that I preferred. Um, Susie Q and runaround Sue when I got into my teen years. But a lot of times it was, uh, Susannah or Susan. In, um, my brother David, since there's four years between us, he often called me suey, which I'm sure anyone would recognize how he liked to taunt me at times, just like brothers do. I actually, um, received my name from my mother, seeing a young girl who was playing nearby her her house, uh, on second Street, and she just was captured by this little girl, and she asked the little girl her name, and she said, Sue Ann Howard. So I was named after Louis Howard, uh, daughter. And my mom just said, when I have a daughter, I'm going to name her Sue Ann. And that's how I got my name. And who was Louis Howard? He, uh, was a newspaper editor in chief at, uh, the messenger index. And long term community member. Okay. All right. Let's go back to living and growing up in Emmett. Um, can you remember what the community was like when you were a child? And what did your family do together, for example? Um, what activities did you do as a, as a as a as a kid? It was probably a very idealistic, uh, place to be. We. Excuse me, had neighbor lived on third Street and we had neighbors, uh, beside us to actually three families of Dillons, um. And up the street we had the Colsons and the Butlers. So we all played together different times. Uh, we had a number of activities that we we did as children because they're between 8 to 10 of us most of the time. We played marbles. We rode bikes around the block and over to fourth Street, played in the canal, found snakes and frogs and shells. We had an empty lot next to one of the residents, the, um, Doris Dillon's home. And so we often played baseball on that, uh, empty, dusty lot. We also did a lot of, um, playing with plastic, small miniature horses. We dug tunnels and played cowboys and Indians. I mean, there wasn't probably anything we didn't do. It was one of those neighbors that would take me to the Emmet Public Library downtown, which was up above First Security Bank on Main Street. And so I also have done a lot of reading in my life. And, um, she's one of the reasons I got started going to public library. We always had books in our house. Mom and dad worked together and dad's business, so we often stayed. My older brother and I stayed with my grandma and grandpa, uh, Walker, who lived on second Street. She helped us learn how to cook and how to, um, you know, practice our reading. And probably the best thing and most, uh, well, it's just a lifelong skill learning how to sew. I, I think first and foremost, the values associated with family and, um, they Where my grandfather was, was not a church goer or anything. Um, Grandma Brown, her her family was of strong faith. And so we had that, you know, involved in a whole culture then with the, uh, brown kids. So there was a tie. Not not just with faith, but with family. And even when there were conflicts, uh, I can look back. And that's pretty normal to not always get along with your siblings. But there was always some kind of resolution. It it might take a little while, but eventually, uh, everyone you know comes to the right, uh, way to see another side to the story. I just think how we grew up there and how much love that we had and the encouragement, whether it be my father who wanted to be involved in electronics, um, Grandma and Grandpa Brown gave him the money, start up money for his first business in 1947. And I found a receipt in his his effects, his paperwork. And it's just here. These people are never really wealthy, but they supported their family with what they did have. And I can see that through, uh, all of dad's relatives, his his siblings, I mean, and I just my mom's family is very similar, uh, in the set of values. Being with the Browns was a happy place. It was very happy. Um, and, you know, a lot of laughing and things. There just was a whole culture of being together that you enjoyed. And as a kid, you always felt safe there. Uh, in that culture growing up and, uh. The the first cousins. And when they come back, it's we're going home. That's the feeling. And you still it's called the home place. And they still feel that way today. Yes. Yes. Okay. Yes. My cousins in Vancouver, we we've got to come to Emmett. You know, they've got to come to Emmett, and then we're going to go out to the home place. You know, so all all the cousins feel that way. I feel that way. Are you nostalgic about the changes that have happened? Oh, Yeah. Hate to say it, but I'm not as progressive as I used to be. Well, um, you've talked a lot about your experiences and your your brown grandparents and how what a happy place that was. And it's still kind of the core of your family, generational relatives, you know. Um, they still want to go back there when they come to Emmett. Um. Are you connected in any way with the orchards even today, at at your age, now that you're an older adult? I hate to say it, but we both are. Yeah. Um. Or is it a thing of the past for you? It's pretty much a thing of the past. I just don't go out and pick. For example, there are still a few trees around, uh, on the property that we could do. How is that property used today? Is it? You said there's still a basic homestead there. They still have a few trees. Are they using it? The property for other, other agricultural things? Uh, not. Not a whole lot anymore. It's kind of just ground that's available. Might be some farming, uh, going on it. Um, and just maintaining the grounds like that. Is the original house still standing? The. Even though it's been updated and remodeled? Probably. But, yes. The original. House is pretty much. Okay. Pretty much all original. I will say that the little house that, uh, was my first home is not on the property anymore. It was finally torn down. There were other people that moved in and out of that. Some of those people who kind of worked or managed on, on the property, uh, in that sense. How many years did you live in that little house? Um, I think I moved to town when I was close to four years old. So it wasn't a long time. And that property then after the after it was rented out or other people lived in it, then it was eventually demolished. And then it's become one of those newer homes that was subdivided out there. Okay. Yes it. Is. All right. Well, let me um, there's a lot more to your family history, including a lot of the things regarding the your religious background. But we'll get to those in another, uh, interview. Let me ask you, thinking about your orchard experience. Um, is there a question or something that I haven't asked that you think is important that maybe we need to include at this point? Well, I'd like to include activities that other activities besides selling fruit on the roadside with my grandmother Brown. She had chickens, so we often went and got eggs while we were there. We she also had, um, a great garden that we could go out and help pick vegetables. But what I remember the most is her flower garden. And it was absolutely beautiful. And we could pick roses or whatever we wanted, uh, for bouquets, and we could take those home. Or she would put them in her, um, house somewhere. That whatever we picked. And I really enjoyed that. There was also an old wheel that they had made into a merry go round for us. And we all loved that. So that was there was an old cherry tree you could climb in. And there was also kind of all in the same location close to the house. There was this, uh, old merry go round the wagon wheel. We spent lots of time, you know, just being kids, being able to run around. But with her, you know, it was the flowers in the garden and the the chickens. And then we could go inside and she'd cook for us. There was nothing that I didn't enjoy more than being able to wake up in the morning in the bed and hear the morning doves and the robins, uh, when I got to stay out there overnight. And we always wanted to stay out there, sometimes I got to stay on my own. Sometimes I stayed with other cousins. My cousin Sharon. Did your grandpa, did he raise any other livestock besides the chickens? They had a few cows, and they might have had a horse at one time. They have a milk cow? Yes, yes. And did your grandma or your grandpa milk the cow? He actually had an old milking machine. He went down there and milked, and my brother went down there to the barn and milked. But I remember her, you know, taking care of the milk, you know, for the, you know, separating it out and stuff like that. I remember her doing a little bit of that. Of course, my father had a, a radio for her. And so there was a kind of a little l shaped her, uh, her sink faced into her utility room. And above the sink there was this old radio. Do you do you remember back in the day what programs she listened to, or maybe even as a family? You listened to the radio before TV became a modern phenomenon? Well, when she listened to it, it probably was more, um, I don't recall for sure, but it seemed like talking or More talking. So it could have been more biblical based, you know? Or it just could have been the announcers talking. Um, what about. You? I felt like I should know the earliest radio station, but I can't think of the call letters right now. Well, we've probably. Unless there's something else you want to share. Um, I want to thank you today for sharing. And what you what you remembered about your grandparents, uh, Brown and and your life early on in the early days when you were just a kid, a little. Kid, a little kid. Um. And it's been a pleasure to interview you. And I'm sure we're going to get to another, um, interview that deals with some of that religious affiliation that your family seems to have had here in the Emmett Valley and in Gem County. So thank you again, Sue. Thank you. Rhonda. Hopefully this works. Well, I don't know how bad it was, but stop. How long is that? Stop recording.

lines.line: # Introduction to the Oral History Project

> The speaker introduces herself as Randa George, volunteering for the Emmett Orchard's oral history project. The interview is conducted with Sue Gunter, a native of Emmett, Idaho, who shares her stories about growing up in the area. The project is supported by the Idaho Historical Society and is part of the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. Sue gives her permission to record the interview.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Oral History Project
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces herself as Randa George, volunteering for the Emmett Orchard's oral history project. The interview is conducted with Sue Gunter, a native of Emmett, Idaho, who shares her stories about growing up in the area. The project is supported by the Idaho Historical Society and is part of the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. Sue gives her permission to record the interview.

lines.line: > Sue was born in a small hospital in Emmett and discusses her family structure, including her parents and siblings. She provides details about her parents' backgrounds, her father's work, and her mother's artistic pursuits. Sue also mentions the influence of her grandparents on her education and career choice.

metadata.title: Early Life and Family Background
metadata.synopsis: Sue was born in a small hospital in Emmett and discusses her family structure, including her parents and siblings. She provides details about her parents' backgrounds, her father's work, and her mother's artistic pursuits. Sue also mentions the influence of her grandparents on her education and career choice.

lines.line: > Sue shares detailed memories of her grandparents' orchard, including the types of fruits grown and her experiences helping in the orchard. She recounts specific stories about her grandfather's activities in the orchard and the family's involvement in its operations.

metadata.title: Memories of the Family Orchard
metadata.synopsis: Sue shares detailed memories of her grandparents' orchard, including the types of fruits grown and her experiences helping in the orchard. She recounts specific stories about her grandfather's activities in the orchard and the family's involvement in its operations.

lines.line: > Sue discusses the operational aspects of the orchard, including the roles of various family members after her grandfather's accident. She explains how the orchard management changed hands within the family and how the property was eventually subdivided and repurposed.

metadata.title: Orchard Operations and Family Changes
metadata.synopsis: Sue discusses the operational aspects of the orchard, including the roles of various family members after her grandfather's accident. She explains how the orchard management changed hands within the family and how the property was eventually subdivided and repurposed.

lines.line: > Sue describes her childhood in Emmett as idealistic, filled with neighborhood games, library visits, and family activities. She recalls the close-knit community and the supportive environment provided by her family, particularly her grandmother who taught her various life skills.

metadata.title: Childhood Experiences and Community Life
metadata.synopsis: Sue describes her childhood in Emmett as idealistic, filled with neighborhood games, library visits, and family activities. She recalls the close-knit community and the supportive environment provided by her family, particularly her grandmother who taught her various life skills.

lines.line: > During her teenage years, Sue was active in school clubs and focused on her education, which was influenced by her family's emphasis on academic achievement. She discusses her high school experiences, friendships, and the supportive community that included caring for classmates with disabilities.

metadata.title: Teenage Years and Educational Pursuits
metadata.synopsis: During her teenage years, Sue was active in school clubs and focused on her education, which was influenced by her family's emphasis on academic achievement. She discusses her high school experiences, friendships, and the supportive community that included caring for classmates with disabilities.

lines.line: # Reflections on Family Values and Orchard Legacy

> Sue reflects on the strong family values instilled by her grandparents and the legacy of the orchard. She emphasizes the importance of family support and the sense of belonging that the orchard provided to her and her extended family.

metadata.title: Reflections on Family Values and Orchard Legacy
metadata.synopsis: Sue reflects on the strong family values instilled by her grandparents and the legacy of the orchard. She emphasizes the importance of family support and the sense of belonging that the orchard provided to her and her extended family.

lines.line: > The interview concludes with plans for future discussions about Sue's religious affiliations and other aspects of her life. The speaker thanks Sue for sharing her memories and experiences, highlighting the importance of preserving such oral histories.

metadata.title: Conclusion and Future Interview Plans
metadata.synopsis: The interview concludes with plans for future discussions about Sue's religious affiliations and other aspects of her life. The speaker thanks Sue for sharing her memories and experiences, highlighting the importance of preserving such oral histories.

children.text: # Introduction to the Oral History Project

> The speaker introduces herself as Randa George, volunteering for the Emmett Orchard's oral history project. The interview is conducted with Sue Gunter, a native of Emmett, Idaho, who shares her stories about growing up in the area. The project is supported by the Idaho Historical Society and is part of the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. Sue gives her permission to record the interview.

> Sue was born in a small hospital in Emmett and discusses her family structure, including her parents and siblings. She provides details about her parents' backgrounds, her father's work, and her mother's artistic pursuits. Sue also mentions the influence of her grandparents on her education and career choice.

> Sue shares detailed memories of her grandparents' orchard, including the types of fruits grown and her experiences helping in the orchard. She recounts specific stories about her grandfather's activities in the orchard and the family's involvement in its operations.

> Sue discusses the operational aspects of the orchard, including the roles of various family members after her grandfather's accident. She explains how the orchard management changed hands within the family and how the property was eventually subdivided and repurposed.

> Sue describes her childhood in Emmett as idealistic, filled with neighborhood games, library visits, and family activities. She recalls the close-knit community and the supportive environment provided by her family, particularly her grandmother who taught her various life skills.

> During her teenage years, Sue was active in school clubs and focused on her education, which was influenced by her family's emphasis on academic achievement. She discusses her high school experiences, friendships, and the supportive community that included caring for classmates with disabilities.
# Reflections on Family Values and Orchard Legacy

> Sue reflects on the strong family values instilled by her grandparents and the legacy of the orchard. She emphasizes the importance of family support and the sense of belonging that the orchard provided to her and her extended family.

> The interview concludes with plans for future discussions about Sue's religious affiliations and other aspects of her life. The speaker thanks Sue for sharing her memories and experiences, highlighting the importance of preserving such oral histories.
